prompt_md: What is the fundamental purpose of `assert` in C++?

options.a: To handle errors from user input gracefully without crashing.
options.b: To document and enforce programmer assumptions — conditions that must always be true if the code is correct.
options.c: To print debug output that is automatically removed in release builds.
options.d: To replace `if` statements with a shorter syntax.

explanation_md: `assert` is for programmer errors — invariants that you guarantee hold if your code is correct. When an assertion fires, it means there is a bug in the calling code, not in the user's input. It is not a user-facing error handler. Options a, c, and d miss the core purpose; option c comes closest but the assertion terminates the program, it doesn't merely print.

Both calls satisfy `lo <= hi` (1 <= 10), so the assertion passes silently. `clamp(5, 1, 10)`: 5 is between 1 and 10, so `val` is returned → `5`. `clamp(0, 1, 10)`: 0 < lo=1, so `lo` is returned → `1`. The assertion only fires if you call `clamp(5, 10, 1)` where lo > hi.

prompt_md: Why is it wrong to put a side-effect expression inside `assert`, like `assert(++x > 0)`?

options.a: Assertions can only contain integer comparisons, not increment operators.
options.b: When `NDEBUG` is defined, the entire `assert` expression is removed, so `++x` never executes in release builds — the variable `x` has a different value depending on build mode.
options.c: The `++x` will always make the assertion pass, so it defeats the purpose.
options.d: Side effects inside `assert` cause undefined behavior in all builds.

explanation_md: When compiled with `NDEBUG` defined (typically in release/optimized builds), the preprocessor removes the entire `assert(...)` call. Any side effects — like `++x`, calling a function that modifies global state, or allocating memory — are also removed. The program then behaves differently in debug vs. release builds, which is a subtle and serious bug.

prompt_md: When should you use `static_assert` instead of `assert`?

options.a: When you want the check to run faster at runtime.
options.b: When the condition involves runtime values like user input or function parameters.
options.c: When the condition can be evaluated at compile time, such as `sizeof(int) >= 4` or a `constexpr` constant.
options.d: When you want the program to continue running even if the check fails.

explanation_md: `static_assert` is evaluated by the compiler before the program runs. It only works on conditions that are constant at compile time — type sizes, `constexpr` values, or compile-time integer arithmetic. Runtime values like parameters or user input are not known at compile time, so `assert` is required for those. Option a is wrong because `static_assert` has zero runtime cost precisely because it runs at compile time.

On virtually all modern platforms, `sizeof(int)` is 4 bytes, which satisfies `>= 2`, so the `static_assert` passes at compile time with no error. The program compiles and runs, printing `4`. If the platform had `sizeof(int) == 1` (extremely unusual), the compilation would fail with the message `"int must be at least 2 bytes"`.
